description_long: This is a modern swivel chair featuring a high, winged backrest and a deep, contoured seat, both upholstered in smooth black leather or faux leather. The chair's exterior shell is crafted from a rich, dark wood veneer, wrapping around the back and sides for a sophisticated contrast. The armrests are integrated into the seat structure, padded and covered in the same black material, providing ergonomic support. The chair sits atop a central metal support column that branches into four slender, splayed legs, finished in matte black. The overall design is sleek and contemporary, with subtle curves and a luxurious appearance. This chair would be well-suited for upscale urban interiors, such as offices, lobbies, or lounges.
description: A luxurious, high-back swivel chair with black leather upholstery, a curved wood shell, and a four-legged metal base. Its modern design features integrated armrests and a winged backrest.
description_view_0: Front view shows the wide, winged backrest, deep seat, and padded armrests, all upholstered in black leather.
description_view_1: Left side view highlights the chair's curved wooden shell, the ergonomic contour of the backrest, and the splayed metal legs.
description_view_2: Rear view displays the smooth, continuous wood veneer shell wrapping around the back, with the metal base visible below.
description_view_3: Right side view mirrors the left, emphasizing the chair's sculpted profile and the integration of the armrest and seat.
attribute_central_support_column: A single, slender, matte black metal column that branches into four splayed legs; fixed height, no visible telescoping or decorative features.
attribute_seat_back_design: High, winged backrest with a gentle curve, upholstered in black leather, featuring subtle vertical creasing and a smooth, continuous wood shell on the exterior.
attribute_armrest_style: Integrated, padded armrests covered in black leather, smoothly connected to the seat and backrest, with a gentle outward curve and no separate attachment.
